title: Facing US export controls, China's DeepSeek plans to make its own chips
gist: DeepSeek is entering the silicon business with data-center inference chips to cut reliance on Huawei and Nvidia amid US export controls, mirroring a broader vertical-integration trend where US firms like OpenAI (with Broadcom's Jalapeño) and Anthropic also pursue custom chips to reduce Nvidia dependence and control the stack amid constrained compute.

insights.claim: DeepSeek is planning to enter the silicon business and has been working on the move for about a year, meeting hardware partners and hiring engineers for the project.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A frontier Chinese LLM maker going vertical into chips signals reduced future addressable market for Nvidia and Huawei in China.

insights.claim: DeepSeek's chip focus is data-center inference rather than training, with the goal of reducing reliance on both Huawei and Nvidia.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Inference-first strategy targets the highest-volume, most cost-sensitive compute segment, the likeliest place custom silicon displaces merchant GPUs.
insights.quote: The focus is on data center chips for inference, not training, and the goal is likely to reduce reliance on both Huawei and Nvidia.

themes.theme: China market structure and export controls

insights.claim: A US export ban has prevented Nvidia from achieving in China the dominance it holds in North America and Europe, while Huawei controls about half of China's data-center chip market.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Quantifies Huawei's ~50% incumbency that DeepSeek, Alibaba, and Baidu are all trying to erode, reshaping China's AI-chip competitive map.
insights.locator: Nvidia is the chipmaker for most

insights.claim: DeepSeek is not alone in trying to enter China's data-center chip market; tech giants Alibaba and Baidu have been making similar moves.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: A crowded field of Chinese in-house silicon efforts intensifies domestic competition and accelerates import substitution away from foreign chips.

insights.claim: OpenAI and Broadcom jointly announced Jalapeño, OpenAI's first chip designed for inference at scale, and Anthropic has been exploring custom chip design without publicly visible milestones yet.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Confirms Broadcom's custom-silicon design win with OpenAI and a broad model-maker push into ASICs, a structural headwind for Nvidia's merchant-GPU margins.
insights.quote: OpenAI and Broadcom jointly announced Jalapeño, the former's first chip designed for inference at scale, just a couple of weeks ago.

insights.claim: Building custom silicon lets AI firms gain Apple-like control over the entire tech stack and secure an advantage in a market where data-center compute access is likely to remain constrained.
insights.speaker: Samuel Axon
insights.so_what: Frames custom chips as a strategic moat and hedge against compute scarcity, not just cost savings, guiding how to value integrated AI players.
insights.quote: it's also a desire to have Apple-like control over the entire tech stack for its products.
